text_content:
Cheetahs do suffer from anxiety a lot. So much so that even in the wild some just stop hunting and die of starvation.
In captivity the anxiety can get even worse.
So giving them a dog companion, especially something like a Golden Retriever whose main goal in life is to make friends and adopt everything and anything can be really beneficial to a Cheetah.
So yes, these big Cats, the largest breed of the Small Cats family actually, do need a puppy of their own.

video_title: When you have to take your baby tiger outside 😂
text_content:
For all those whom are concerned and wondering about why this young man has a tiger is, because he rescued Romeo from being sold to the circus. Romeo, the tiger I do believe will be given sanctuary once he grows bigger into his adult stage and be with his own kind. He is not a pet, but this young man considers him family as well as Romeo. Romeo thinks of him as a parental figure and family. The day they have to part will be devastating, but once he adjusts to his new environment it will be for the best. Of course this young man will visit when he can and the reunions will be amazing. Edit: Thanks for the love to those who liked, but this will be my one and only edit because apparently I have to be a science teacher to get the... "not so intelligent" people out of my replies, so here it goes. 
A panther is not an animal. 
The term "panther" is a genus, or class of animals, describing big cats. A "Black panther" is not a specific animal. It's a member of the panthera genus with melanism. Melanism makes you black, hence the name "Black panther". 
It's a description, not an animal. A black lion is a black panther, a black tiger is a black panther, in the same way that a black jaguar is a black panther. text_content:
We live in large subdivision located in fair size town in the Idaho mountains. We’ve had mountain lions found bedding under blue spruce trees in front yards in the middle of winter .  One took two cats in one night which could be extremely dangerous for young children and small adults who walk past within four feet of that same tree .   I’ve mentioned this to the neighbors and suggested that the branches at ground level should be removed to at least five feet above the ground but nothing has happened so I’m going to the city .
We’ve had similar situations in nearby subdivisions but instead of cats they were going after full grown elk , dragging them underneath a tree ten feet from my friends driveway and feeding on the elk for most of the winter and because of the deep snow we’re completely unseen .Next spring he trimmed the branches up to eight feet high. They have similar videos of a lion coming up to the picture windows eyeing their two dogs .

text_content: I'm puzzled why people in the comments are criticizing the security guard, when in fact, he was trying to save numerous lives. The tragic incident of the escaped lion highlights the importance of responsible animal ownership. While it's tempting to blame the security guard for shooting the lion, I firmly believe he made the right call to protect human lives. The real culprit, in my opinion, is the lion's owner, who kept a wild animal in captivity. By doing so, he not only put his own life at risk but also the lives of those around him. The lion's eventual escape and tragic demise were inevitable consequences of its unnatural confinement. The security guard's actions, though unfortunate, were necessary to prevent further harm to the community. Ultimately, this incident serves as a reminder of the dangers of keeping wild animals in domestic environments.

text_content: My one story about jaguars:  I was at a nature park in Southern Mexico, and came upon an enclosure labeled "jaguar."  It was a large enclosure with the natural, thick, local limestone rainforest habitat, but there wasn't any part of the enclosure that was blocked from view.  I looked and looked but couldn't see any jaguars in there, so I figured I'd had the misfortune to come when the enclosure was empty for cleaning, maintenance, etc.  I was just about to walk away when the black jaguar who had been lounging on a tree limb stretched, yawned, and repositioned itself.  It was literally right in front of me, not 20 feet away, and I had been looking straight at it for at least a minute.  But it blended so well with the broken sunlight and random shadow pattern of the jungle canopy that I hadn't been able to see it until it moved--it had been completely invisible to my eyes and brain, even though I had been actively looking for it.  If we'd have been in the bush, I could have walked right into that enormous murder cat and never have seen it, and obviously, it could have killed me before I had any idea I was even in any danger.  It was an amazing demonstration of how effective their camouflage is in their natural environment, and a sobering demonstration of something a local jungle guide told me:  if you walk into jaguar territory and walk out alive, it's because the jaguar decided to allow you to live that day.

text_content: What Jon says here confirms what I have heard from other sources.  Setting aside the problem of how to ethically purchase a healthy cheetah cub, the biggest challenge with maintaining a healthy cheetah in captivity is properly sourcing their diet.  If cheetahs do not receive the proper mix of proteins and other nutrients, they will experience various chronic health problems and experience shortened life spans.  From a personality and instinct perspective, cheetahs are the easiest big cat to adapt to a human environment.  Far easier in fact than tigers, lions, leopards, jaguars or pumas, who are potentially very dangerous even when hand-raised from infancy by their humans.  If socialized from cubs to interact with humans, cheetahs will bond and imprint on their human family, and interact like an overgrown house cat.  That said, they instinctually want to mark their territory -- and a 75- to 125-pount cheetah can produce a lot of urine.  They also want to claw and chew inanimate objects.  These are not "indoor" cats; they need to be kept outside a human home, with access to appropriate open space for exercise and play.  At best, having a pet cheetah is an expensive luxury that requires a significant input of cash, time and effort that is beyond the reach of most human beings to provide.

text_content: for context, cheetahs are naturally nervous (highly alert) animals. because in the wild, they share habitats with not only animals in heavier weight class, but also have to deal with pack animals like hyenas stealing their food all the time. growing up in capativity away from their peers can exacerbate the anxiety. so adopted cheetahs usually are paired with a dog from breeds that are just a ball of positivity all the time, like retreivers and border collie etc, to help them chill out essentially. retrievers are usually selected to pair with cheetahs because both golden retriever and labrador retriever can have very similar coat colour to cheetahs and zoo keepers/animal rescuers think that helps with forming a bond quickly between the two species. but no evidence suggests that's the case. there are many cheetahs raised alongside border collies/shepards etc all grew up to be confident animals. cheetahs are also probably the easiest animal to form domestic cat-like bonds with humans among all african cats. i don't think they care about the colour and shape of their friend. they just want a friend to grow up with.

text_content:
I would be careful about having one as a pet, but the short also isn't entirely wrong. Humans were actually in the process of domesticating the cheetah up until about the 8th century. As such, they're sort of "half-domesticated", which is why they have such issues with anxiety and they still have those genes that say "yeah humans are kinda cool". They were living near people for thousands of years, until one day that stopped happening. Imagine everyone just abandoned their dogs one day and thousands of years were allowed to pass. The resulting descendants probably wouldn't be domesticated anymore, but would probably not be completely wild either as they'd still have that genetic response to humans on some level.
This is also why they get along with dogs and it calms their anxiety, another holdover from the time they were almost household pets themselves.

video_title: Would a Capuchin Monkey Make a Good Pet? | Pets Rated 😲 #capuchinmonkey #animals
video_description: Smart, curious, and incredibly social—capuchin monkeys can be adorable and challenging at the same time. Could they make a ...
text_content:
Unfortunately, like many NHP’s, Capuchins are marketed to many as kids that don’t grow up. Here’s the thing; all animals grow up and mature. And these guys want some independence and companionship.
They’re toddlers with knives for teeth so be prepared for that.
Not to mention how they’re acquired. Primates all have the planet’s longest time with their mothers before they’re mature, at least 3-4 years. Taking them away, which when poached will often result in the mother’s death and those of the troop members, causes a lot of psychological issues. Unfortunately, baby monkeys are the main marketable ones on social media so there’s a lot of money to just keep these monkeys for a few years in some countries and then, when they’re too big to be cute, either release them into the wild with no experience or.., something truly soul destroying.
In countries they’re not native to, they’re more expensive so people do keep them. The laws do vary though. Come next year and the rules for owning one will be heavily raised in the UK.

Did I say one? That’s the other thing about all NHP’s aside from maybe orangutans where the males are very solitary; they are incredibly social animals that, in the wild, spend their days roaming and foraging. You can’t just have one or two. They need a group. And, as for space, they need more than we can ever give them.
That giving them to sanctuaries part does forget one other thing; in countries like the US and UK, the sanctuaries are pretty much full because there’s no real naturally occurring wild population in the countries to rehabilitate them to live in. They’re essentially retirement homes for creatures that, usually given up around the age of 7, will be stuck there for about 30 years if they have a space. Many of which may not even know how to interact with their own species in subjects like troop dynamics because they were taken from their mothers before they could learn.

Sorry for the rant. Just wanted to give some extra information about these little guys because poaching and the wild animal trade are pretty foul.

Now, I know the main argument in the US will be that some have probably seen a Capuchin be an animal assistant for severely disabled people. Whilst that was true, they’re no longer listed as that category of animal for many reasons and the charity now looks after them all full time in their retirement, now looking towards robotics instead.

video_title: Would a Capuchin Monkey Make a Good Pet? | Pets Rated 😲 #capuchinmonkey #animals
video_description: Smart, curious, and incredibly social—capuchin monkeys can be adorable and challenging at the same time. Could they make a ...
text_content:
Yep. You probably need to know the difference between a Capuchin and Macaque monkeys first. Then try and speak from a place of authority and expertise. Not knowing the simple difference in two breeds of monkeys probably hurts the message that I believe you are trying to get across. Monkeys are an amazing little life form. Their eyes, faces, little human like hands, and curious playful nature are hard to resist. Their similarity to humans is amazing and why so many people enjoy watching them. The fact we find them so adorable and want to hug and take care of all of them is something programmed deep within our being. Since similar to humans, both male and female humans feel the way they do about them because babies propagate and ensure the survival of a species. The will of the cells to multiply and copy themselves drives all living things. However, that cannot occur if the babies don't live and survive long enough to repeat the once again. So, monkey babies being similar in appearance to that of human babies, subconsciously drives us to protect, provide, and comfort them. The owning of a primate, great or small, as a pet isn't the best for the monkeys. These little beings were designed by the creator to florish in their natural habitats. Despite the fact humans are encroaching into the monkeys home, more on that in a bit, they still belong in their home. As someone who rescues and rehabilitates primates whenever that very thing occurs, most of our rescues don't come from a situation of the little guys being injuried from saws or bulldozers. Instead they came from uniformed and in most cases good intentioned people trying to make them their pet.
 Typically, they will have everything they think a monkey needs. A very large enclosure, the right foods, toys, treats, and lots of love. However, even that is not enough to make sure these wonderful beings have the opportunity to live long fulfilling monkey lives. With baby monkeys, depending on their age and breed, have very strenuous feeding cycles. A capuchin monkey around 2 weeks old needs to be fed every 2 to 3 hours, day and night. At this age, they are usually fed a specialized milk replacer or formula via a small 1cc syringe to prevent aspiration. Thats day and night. Most people struggle with that sort of a feeding cycle with a human baby let alone a fury one. This must be followed up with many monkey babies by having to rub their genital area with a warm, damp tissue after every feeding to stimulate urination and defecation. Wow, not something people are accustomed too. Then the baby capuchin must also be kept in an incubator or at a consistent warm temperature (85°F or above) as they cannot regulate their own body temperature at this age. Just to name a few. 

 Whenever people get frustrated, busy, have a human baby, lose a job, or suffer one of a litany of possible lifestyle changes such as a drastic income change or they can no longer dedicate the amount of time needed to give monkeys the attention or care they need for a healthy life. The other big one we run into is that people aren't prepared for is when the cute babies turn into older monkeys. Sometimes a monkey can get aggressive at varying times in their lives including hormone changes and that desire to mate. This isn't the monkeys fault because this is how they are designed deep in their DNA. That isn't something you should punish as this is completely natural. They have similar emotions and feelings just like we do and can get mad, depressed, stressed out and can even get very possessive with their owner. Whenever they get jealous or possessive those little human like arms, hands, and fingers become poop flinging, fist-pounding, object throwing expressions of those feelings. Thats when they either get mistreated, abandoned, and neglected. 

Regardless of the reason their owners no longer can keep them and places like the one a work at come into play. Although their owners may have started out with good intentions, spent untold fortunes on an enclosure as big as some people's entire house, provided vet care, love them, and will end up missing them just like anyone would whenever they say goodbye to a family member. The results are the same. The monkey suffers and doesn't get the opportunity to live like they would if they were left alone in their natural habitat. Thats when organizations and sanctuaries get involved. Sadly, there are far too many stories I could share about what our little fury buddies must have went through. Some of them had been living in the most disgusting and vile environments you could imagine. The most heartbreaking thing of all wasn't just the abhorrent state of their physical health but their mental state as well. Abandon, mistreated, and in the care of total strangers these monkeys have had their hearts crushed. All they know is how to be a monkey and a few party tricks they have been taught to put on shows for money. They don't fully understand the minds of mankind. Many times their owners have had them since they were babies. All they know is you! Now, for at least the second time in their life, that pillar of stability, security, and  consistency is gone. They again have lost both things suddenly and they think it was them that caused this. Please, use this data to make an informed decision.

text_content:

I have learned something from you all & it has changed my opinion. No, this isn't "cute", it's actually quite sad. To think what this creature has been thru/endured already in his young life makes my heart ache. Poor thing.

Pica should be with his mother & own kind. He's a wild animal - not a pet & definitely not a tiny human. No way he'd be walking on 2 legs without being coerced to do so - it simply wouldn't happen otherwise.

text_content:
In person, I've seen gibbons brachiating a few times, and was always totally jealous! I've always loved climbing trees, but am old now, and can hopefully live/swing vicariously through watching them! Lovely furry people and, sadly, been done a terrible wrong by the dominant apes of the planet! 
As an old person, I'm sometimes ashamed of the people we used to be- I remember going into a pet store and there were small monkeys in metal cages, screaming and climbing about. My brother and I were children, and wanted a "pet monkey," but my Dad said no.  
He was much smarter than we were, and had much more empathy! 
Oh those poor little creatures!

video_title: Man Lives With His Two Pet Gibbons
video_description: ID: 3447750 ONSCREEN CREDIT - @eldar.gadjiev This man lives with two pet gibbons in his home in Moscow, Russia. Designer ...
text_content:
There is a gibbon sanctuary not far from my house. Even though gibbons are beyond adorable, fascinating, and even though humans and gibbons are both primates, people CANNOT be replacement gibbons. Gibbons thrive in their natural habitat, NOT as expensive novelty pets. Gibbons kept in captivity often develop behaviors that their owners can't handle when they grow up. Pet baby gibbons are frequently separated from their mothers before they are ready to be independent just for someone's amusement. 
The owner of the gibbon sanctuary by my house told me that you can tell which gibbons were separated from their mothers as infants because they will suck their thumbs. The gibbons at this sanctuary are descendants of generations of captive pet gibbons. The sanctuary is a full time, intense job and labor of love for the lady who keeps the sanctuary. People have absolutely no idea how labor intensive these adorable tiny gibbons will grow up to be. Even the most attuned human will absolutely NOT be a gibbon mother who can teach a gibbon how to be a gibbon. This baby gibbon's vocalizations were tragic, because this baby gibbon is instinctually calling out for other gibbons to socialize with, especially its mother. 
As much as I adore gibbons, keeping this tiny baby gibbon as an amusing pet is beyond narcissistic and short sighted. Gibbons in a house will always need a diaper, because I have never seen or heard if one who could be potty trained.

text_content: I suspect a lot of the staged monkey video channels  from Vietnam to be a showcase for the exotic pet black market.  The "caretakers" on these channels seem to be related to each other, and i suspect with a larger organization.  They make it look like it's easy and fun to have a monkey as a pet, and that increases the demand.  The monkeys generally disappear before the age of three, often without any explanation.  For many of them, they are abandoned in the forest when they get less cute or too difficult to manage, but i think the best trained monkeys are certainly sold on the black market.  These monkeys already have lots of admirers who want to adopt them, and these criminals would'nt miss the opportunity to make a last very big amount of money with them.

video_title: Slow Loris Tickle (Sonya The Tickle Lover)
video_description: UPDATE: http://www.youtube.com/DmitrySergeyev - the owner of this video. Please, visit his channel. Her name is Sonya and she ...
text_content:
Although the actions depicted in this video are viewed as harmless, animal experts assert that these animals experience high levels of stress from being tortured during the course of such "performances": Dr. Anna Nekaris, an anthropologist who specializes in slow-loris research at Oxford Brookes University states that the loris willingness to be held is not because they are comfortable but rather it is part of their defense mechanism — to be still and silent when they feel threatened. The only reason the loris isn't biting the person holding it in the video is because it has had its teeth cruelly ripped out by illegal traders who poach them from their natural rain forest habitat. The teeth are removed because the loris can deliver a toxic bite. The reason the tickled slow loris is seen raising its arms (a sign of distress) is because it is making an attempt to defend itself by reaching its toxic gland: The slow loris is the world's only poisonous primate. Its venom is stored in an elbow patch. When it is feeling threatened, the slow loris will raise its arms above its head in a diamond shape, suck in the poison from its elbow patch, then mix it around in its mouth before delivering the toxic bite. These animals are also often in pain because they are nocturnal and not equipped to deal with extended periods of daylight. In addition to causing a high level of stress in the animal, this video also encourages the illegal trade of slow lorises: The popularity of slow loris videos has led to an increase in the number of people wanting to have a slow loris as a pet. And while it may appear cute, the animal in the video is suffering and so is the entire species. Slow lorises are shoved into plastic 
crates and bags; their teeth are clipped out with pliers or wire cutters with no anesthesia, no aftercare, no medicine and most die; multiple animals are crammed into tiny cages where they wound each other with their venomous bites. Large percentages of captured slow lorises die due to inhumane, overcrowded, stressful conditions during transportation often with the bodies of others that have died on the journey. This painful process of mutilation not only often kills the animals via blood loss or infection, but also prevents rescued lorises from being released back into the wild. Not only does the slow loris pet trade cause unimaginable suffering, it is also the biggest threat to the survival of the species, which is in serious danger of extinction. All eight species of slow lorises are considered “threatened” and the Javan slow loris is one of the 25 most endangered primates worldwide. So if you have a heart, please do not like or share this video.

text_content: Because of their "cuteness", videos of pet slow lorises are some of the mostly frequently watched animal-related viral videos on YouTube.[60][123] In March 2011, a newly posted video of a slow loris holding a cocktail umbrella had been viewed more than two million times, while an older video of a slow loris being tickled had been viewed more than six million times.[131] According to Nekaris, these videos are misunderstood by most people who watch them, since most do not realize that it is illegal in most countries to own them as pets and that the slow lorises in the videos are only docile because that is their passive defensive reaction to threatening situations.[123][131] Despite frequent advertisements by pet shops in Japan, the World Conservation Monitoring Centre reported only a few dozen slow lorises were imported in 2006, suggesting frequent smuggling.[83] Slow lorises are also smuggled to China, Taiwan, Europe, Russia, the United States, and Saudi Arabia for use as pets.[130][120][131]

A few things about the slow loris, they’re solitary animals. 

The noise it is emitting is actually a hiss. 
When slow lorises are terrified their first reaction is to freeze as a defense. 
When that does not work they will hiss or growl. 
Of that does not work they launch at their attacker. 
The loris in this video as actively trying to bite this person which in accordance to primatologists is its last resort. 

Lorises also have their teeth pulled when they are shipped for captivity as illegal pets. This is so when they do bite they do not transfer their venom to the victim. 

All of this is important information because in short, this slow loris is in Hell and being tortured.

text_content:
I'd prefer that people require training/permits to acquire and raise certain common "exotic" pet species.  
Sadly, with the tragic trend of infant macaques in Cambodia and various parts of Indonesia being abducted and sold, these remarkable species are being exploited, tormented, starved, and abused on film by sadists who appeal to those who prefer to watch this abuse in their free time.  
Monkeys are extremely social and they require more care, attention, and affection than most people are willing (and able) to offer...  
As much as I would love to experience the challenge of raising a monkey from infancy, I also know that my family and my job require the most of my attention.  If I couldn't provide the best possible life for an animal, I would not acquire the animal.  
Pets are cohabitating companions, they are not accessories...

text_content: Monkeys are not meant to be "pets." It is in dogs' DNA to share our lives, but no other animal shares this. We are meant to live in harmony with the earth and her wild things, not to dominate them. A monkey is meant to live in a house just like a human is not meant to live on the ocean floor. Can it be done? Yep. Doesnt mean it SHOULD be done. Allow wild things to stay wild, please. The world is not ours, it belongs to all the wild creatures. We're lucky enough to share it for a tiny amount of time. Not sure where we got the idea this planet is ours but it's time to change our thinking.

text_content:
Bushbaby as pets?
The tiny "Nagapie" (night monkey in Afrikaans) are at risk due to humans wanting to keep them as inappropriate pets. 
 
Cute and cuddly looking but did you know they mark their territories by urinating into a cupped hand and then rubbing this on the feet, bushbabies spread their scent wherever they move.  They are also only active at night and need a very specialised diet.
​
Many species of nocturnal primates are increasingly threatened by illegal collecting for the pet trade. When transported outside of their natural range and climate, many bushbabies die. This is partly because they are adapted to a specific climate and landscape, but also because each species has a very specific diet, which they typically do not receive when they are kept as pets, by inexperienced keepers.
 
Furthermore the incorrect diet will result in mal-formed bones, kidney and liver damage, obesity and psychological impairments.
​
Bushbabies are very prone to respiratory problems if the correct temperatures are not maintained.
 
New owners often grow tired quickly; these are nocturnal primates and owners often get bored of them sleeping during the day and do not give enough gum or  adequet insects needed to sustain them at night.  When the novelty wears off the animals are often dumped in the nearest veld. Where they often do not survive as they do not know the landscape; dehydration and starvation.
​
It is AGAINST THE LAW TO KEEP INDIGENOUS WILD ANIMALS AS PETS AND UNETHICAL.
​
If you see someone with an indigenous wild animals as a pet, get in touch.  Or call your local SPCA.

Many species of nocturnal primates are increasingly threatened by illegal collecting for the pet trade. When transported outside of their natural range and climate, many bushbabies die. This is partly because they are adapted to a specific climate and landscape, but also because each species has a very specific diet, which they typically do not receive when they are kept as pets, by inexperienced keepers.
 
Furthermore the incorrect diet will result in mal-formed bones, kidney and liver damage, obesity and psychological impairments.
​
Bushbabies are very prone to respiratory problems if the correct temperatures are not maintained.
 
New owners often grow tired quickly; these are nocturnal primates and owners often get bored of them sleeping during the day and do not give enough gum or  adequet insects needed to sustain them at night.  When the novelty wears off the animals are often dumped in the nearest veld. Where they often do not survive as they do not know the landscape; dehydration and starvation.
​
It is AGAINST THE LAW TO KEEP INDIGENOUS WILD ANIMALS AS PETS AND UNETHICAL.
​
If you see someone with an indigenous wild animals as a pet, get in touch.  Or call your local SPCA.

video_title: Living with an African Grey: What It’s Really Like After 10 Years
video_description: Living with an African Grey: What It's Really Like After 10 Years We are going to answer 10 questions that you should think about ...
text_content:
I have a 13 year old male CAG. He has been “my” bird up until just recently.  He’s now taken a shine to my daughter.  😊 which is fine with me. Because even though I was his person, he’d bite me all the time. And, I’m not talking love bites. He bit for blood. I have so many scars!  

These birds are so smart! They really shouldn’t be pets.  They belong out in the wild to be free to fly and be birds.  I also have a Senegal parrot.  He’s a very clingy bird that just wants to be with me all the time. He’s smart as well.  But, I think all parrots are a little psycho, they aren’t completely domesticated.  They are still wild and deserve to be free. Again, they deserve to be free to fly.  I think that’s why people give up parrots. They don’t realize the work that parrots are and they don’t want to deal with them.  Or, sadder still, they keep them in dark rooms, feed them and forget them.

text_content:
As an Aussie, we see a lot of escaped/released pet cockatoos - you can tell  because they're usually solo not in a flock and they're extra social and less fearful of people. We have one that occasionally visits us - it's very pointed about telling us what it likes in our garden! Any time we plant parsley, it's mysteriously ripped out and usually deposited onto our downstairs neighbour's balcony! More than once, we've had to knock on their door and ask if we could our plant back please! 

In a flock, they're one of the funniest birds ever - imagine a small child trying to do acrobatics...whilst eating...and talking... They're upside down, food in one claw, usually screeching up a storm and often trying to shoo another cockatoo away with their wings. Then times that by about 100 and put it in the most publicly visible place. It is a magnificent and hilarious sight to behold!

We adore them. They will find a tree in the early evening, making a huge amount of noise about it. Watching them fly over is spectacular. There are selected up to three cockatoo’s for sentry duty at the top of the tree. With my binoculars I can often see that they sleep on the job. Other than humans they don’t have a predator. They make us laugh everyday with their acrobatics. To adopt a rescue is like having a two to three year old child forever. They live long lives and usually have an attachment to one person, in your case your husband. 🕊️🇦🇺😀🏡

text_content:
Editing for YouTube, especially if you do it yourself and speaking in a non-native language is really hard!  Great work putting this piece together.  Great Green Macaws are one of our most cherished species of Macaw at Flight Club Foundation.  In fact, the illegal trade out of Columbia brought a Great Green from the Darien Gap to the United States in a smuggling ring.  My friend began a reintroduction program in the Darien Gap and now 12 pairs are successfully raising chicks.  It's been a 30 year program almost nearing its end.  

My question is in regard to numbers.  I know Costa Rican Great Green and Ecuadorean Great Greens are really the last two strongholds, is my understanding.  Down to hundreds left?  My understanding is there are a few thousand left, but the increased pressure from colonization is really the biggest issue (deforestation).  I also know the local pet trade is a serious issue since the birds are available and the people are living right there.  

The devastation I saw in that sattelite image was profound and needs to be shared far, far, far more.  I work with Dr. George Olah, PhD and his work discovering the root causes of worldwide parrot decline. In addition, I know Janice Boyd is an avid Great Green advocate.   I did not see the name of your sanctuary, perhaps I missed it?  

I

text_content: Like the person from WA I am also an Aussie who lives in a suburb close to the Blue Mountains. I also have mobs of Sulphur Crested Cockatoos, Corellas, Rainbow Lorikeets, Magpies, Magpie Larks, two ibis, two Ravens, two Little Blackbirds, two Kookaburras and A male Blue Satin Bowerbird and his mate the Green Satin Bowerbird who come to my home morning and evening for a feed of natural wild birdseed. Now and again I see a pair of Black Cockatoos fly over and I have a very large Owl who comes and lands on my clothesline in the night every August which is our winter. video_description: If your Hyacinth Macaw wants to play! #play #playful #happy #cute #cuteanimals.
text_content:
Beautiful baby...her/his flight feathers don't appear to be fully open yet!
I raised B&G, Green Wing, and Military macaws for some years before taking them all down to Panama, releasing them on Isla Coiba before it was established as a National Park in 1991.  I worked with the Panamanian government throughout the '70s--'80s doing research toward establishing the island as a national park...Isla Coiba has the largest density of macaws anywhere in Latin America.  There were times I saw flocks of over 25 macaws flying about this pristine (uninhabited) island. My research there, and other locals was basis for my PhD...until politics intervened my dream was re-establishing macaws in their original range throughout the West Indies.
I've some great tales of rescuing baby Hyacinth macaws from giant trees (illegally) cut down in the Northern Pantanal region...turning them over to indigenous elders who then entrusted them to local children already well accustomed to rearing parrots/macaws...almost got shot several times by parrot poacher, too!  
You hyacinth is clever, beautiful and will give you and your family life-long joy...take care of and protect "her"!

I cant remember which valley it was, but i remember it was one with no existing population, but also had a very active pest control programme. text_content:
Those are depressingly tiny cages. We really need to stop keeping parrots in cages barely wide enough to stretch their wings or walk more than a few steps. Especially something like macaws need to FLY. Even the reptile world is miles ahead of the bird world in giving their animals enriching, naturalistic enclosures with lots of room.
And I don't know why it is, but in 15+ years, I have never seen a pet palm cockatoo that's not a sickly pale pink. In zoos and in the wild, they have blood red cheeks. There must be something wrong with their diets as pets, and I would be very interested to see the lifespan of pet palms.

(Nitpicky, but hawkheads aren't Amazons in any way. They're from South America, but so are macaws and conures. Hawkheads' closest relatives are caiques.)

video_description: The small yet mighty bog turtle is: New Jersey's State Reptile Extremely cute & rare Critically endangered Today, New ...
text_content: I’ve been herping for many years and the Bog (or Muhlenberg’s) Turtle is the ONLY species native to the Tri-State region (NY, NJ, CT) that I’ve never seen in their native habitat. I have only seen adults and hatchlings in one place; the Stony Brook Lab & Fish Hatchery on Long Island, NY. They have several pair in a breeding program because they are a critically endangered species on CITES. Their small size made them ideal pets and during the early 1900s they were over collected for the pet trade. This in combination with habitat destruction led to their near extinction in the wild.

text_content:
I'm fixing this list 
5- northamerican spotted turtles, make good pets. But do to the fact they're illegal in many places, they can't place higher. Otherwise, they might have made the 1st on this list. They can kinda pricy also but thats probably because so few people work with them. Other than that, the video did ok with these. 
.
4-Map turtles- they're commonly available, usually for similar prices to species that don't make good pets like sliders. Stay a reasonable size, although they can be shy.
.
3-Painted turtles- the video actually did a good job for these.
.
2-African Dwarf mud turtle- these would be in 1st if they weren't so expensive.
.
1- Most American Musk/Mud turtles- aside from a few species that literally have Giant in the name, pretty much all of them make a good choice as a pet. My husband and I have spent 2 spring/summer seasons rescuing turtles off the roads in our area. We have saved around 30 turtles of differing species and have even had to help some of them heal from injuries and malnutrition before releasing them back into the wild near where we found them, though we made sure they were a good distance from the road when releasing them. 

I am proud to say we have helped these amazing creatures and hope there are others doing similar things in their areas as well. Just stopping to help a turtle get across the road safely is a good thing to do to help lessen the number of injured or killed turtles. All it takes is a few minutes of your time and a small amount of effort on our parts. 

Thank you for another great video Clint. Can’t wait for the next one. ☺️💕

text_content:
Clint ,
I grew up (age 2 to 18) on a 27 acre farm in Jackson, N.J. , bordered on the south side by a brook(the north branch of the Metedeconk River.
Across the road from our Victorian farmhouse was another old farm with a house dating back to the mid 18th century.
It had been farmed by the Clayton family for many generations and many generations of children had played with Box Turtles which frequented the wild strawberries growing  there.
It wasn't uncommon to find turtles with "plastrons". with initials on them carved there by these children.
In the late fifties, I was visiting the field and found a huge box turtle.
On it's plastron was carved,  "Lt. Opey Y Co. 189?
I took a piece of brown paper and copied the carving just as it was on the turtle.
 I almost brought it home, almost! It had survived for so long I did not want to endanger it in any way.
I never researched him, but Lt. OPey may have been a Spanish American or possibly a First World War Veteran.
So, I've always wondered jut how old was that Turtle(Terrapin) to have had that much carving still very readable,
when Opey left his message?
I'll never know,  but  that was the most amazing Terrapin encounter I ever had.
Cheers,
Rik Spector

text_content:
Used to find many in the suburban subdivision I used to live. We found 3 or 4 babies when I was a child. Last summer I found 3 in the same month in August, all within a couple hundred yard range. One was a smaller mature mature female, then there was a juvenile female and a juvenile male. All very healthy and vibrant. Sadly, the small patch of woodland that I used to walk daily for 20 years was ripped up and destroyed the following fall. The county needed to maintenance on a lot of underground water lines that passed through the forest, so now it's all gone. I used to see great blue herons, foxes, beavers, muskrats, deer, red tailed hawks, geese, woodland mallards, box turtles, occasional common snapping turtle, cooter turtles, other basking turtles, bullfrogs, raccoons and all sorts of other things there despite the woodland being surrounded on all sides by subdivisions. All gone now.

I live somewhere more rural and remote, but haven't seen a fraction of the wildlife I used to see there. It feels like a part of me was taken when they tore all those trees down and will never return.

Do you think he will survive living in a protected place with other turtles and tortoises? He has lots of brush, a large creek nearby, sandy soil, and plenty of open grass and other low vegetation and insects to munch on.

text_content: Daughter found an injured box turtle about a month ago.  She looks like something tried to eat it.  The left eye is missing and has a crack in her shell.  She took it somewhere and was told that the eye was infected.  I tried to bring it back to health by giving it some penicillen.  It wasn't able to open it's mouth.  After a week with medicine, it was able to eat the grounded up bugs/worms that I made mush out of.  the next week, she was eating lettuce, carrots and some fruits.  Hopefully she will be able to eat live worms and more soon.  I got her to my home and she now has a great home that PetSmart and another turtle owner helped me set up.  I am afraid that she will never be able to be returned to the wild, but I take her outside everyday so that she can feel the grass and wind. Love my new pet.

text_content: Since moving to southern VA I have saved so many that get in the road, especially after it rains. The other day I pulled over to get one out of the road and while I was getting out of my car someone ran right over it. I heard the crunching of its shell & it literally broke my heart. It wasn’t even fully in the road yet so the person was either not paying ANY attention or did it on purpose. It has bothered me for over a week now. A few days ago as I was weed eating an overgrown area of tall grass around my home I saw a yellow & brown creature deep in the grass. I jumped back thinking it was likely a snake but upon closer inspection it was a turtle!! I stopped weed eating and left it alone. I’ve since seen it a couple more times around the yard. I hope it stays around. I would never want to cage it & I wasn’t sure what to do to help it thrive in my yard. We are in the country with plenty of woods so hopefully it will do well. I know that God brought that turtle to me because of what happened the other day. God’s creation is so beautiful & amazing & most people don’t even see or acknowledge it. Every day as I drive I am in awe of God’s creation all around us!

text_content:
40 some odd years ago I was living in DC with a friend who would pick these guys up off the parkway (so they wouldn't get killed) and brought them home to our top floor brownstone. We went to the Library of Congress to find out how to care for them. Cat food with bones and eyes (yuck) worked well. They ran free and I just put papers where they liked to potty.
When I headed back to Cali, we released them in a marshy area. They couldn't run away from each other fast enough. I was dissapointed they didn't want to remain buddies.

text_content: About 40 years ago I was going to high school after Thanksgiving break. Here in the Mid-Atlantic it was just below freezing and a freezing drizzle was coming down. As I started my car at about 6:45 am I saw a lump in the middle of the street. I stopped to look at this little lump. It was a sweet little box turtle, probably fully grown. It wasn't moving, it was tucked in its shell. I wasn't entirely certain it was alive at first. I picked him (no idea if this is accurate) up and took him into the house. We placed him in a milk crate in a cold area of the house. He stayed with us until the weather broke. I would set him outside in his crate during the day and bring him in at night. It would get down near freezing at night and as warm as 50sF in the day.  One day in late April, the crate was over on its side and he was gone. I hope he lived a long turtle life after that.

video_title: My Box Turtle Care Guide! How I Take Care Of My Oldest Pet (Learn From My Mistakes)
video_description: My Box Turtle Franklin is the oldest pet I have! At 22 years old I've been caring for him the majority of my life and have learned a ...
text_content: I was given a 3 toed box turtle 35 years ago by a coworker whose daughter was abusing it. She told me it ate wet Purina Cat Chow. I found that even if I put the food out every day, it only seemed interested about once a week so I only put out food once a week after that. Also, I found that since it has free range of the house, it can still sense Summer and Winter and it sort of hibernates in the Winter and doesn't eat. I still have a 2" deep water dish out at all times if it wakes up and needs to hydrate. I had read that the red eyed ones were males and brown eyed ones were females. I got confirmation of that when one day while the food was out and it was basking under the lamp that I put over the food dish. He was "fanning". His junk was all hanging out of his cloaca. It scared the shit out of me but when I got close to check it out, he sucked it all back in. Let me tell ya, there's an awful lot of junk in that trunk. A little research told me what was going on there. Considering what our life is like here, I'd say it is the easiest pet I have ever had. Even in the Summer, I could be gone for a week and no harm done. After 35 years, I must be doing something right. He was full grown size when I got him so I have no idea how old he is. He could drop dread tomorrow for all I know. I may need to get his beak trimmed. It's getting quite long but he can still eat the Cat Chow with no problem.

I wish I was able to identify my old Box Turtle. 
By all appearances you would have thought she was a Northeastern American box turtle but she didn't quite match.
 She had an all dark brown shell with no markings on it, a tan bottom to her shell,  her skin was all tannish lite green with orange spots on her front legs only, and green eyes.

I rescued her years ago at a state park. I was walking along a path and a box turtle came out of the bushes and walked up to me, she tried to climb up my leg wanting to be picked up, when I picked her up she did not suck into her shell, she had all of her extremities hanging out and had her head craned around to look at me. I also will mention that this was the beginning of November when I found her, I found out by an old couple that seen a family had released her into the wild, in the beginning of the winter at that.

text_content: Living so close to the Mississippi here in La Crosse, we occasionally see turtles crossing roads to get to good egg-laying sites further inland, and further into human habitat.  One or two seasons ago, we saw a mom turtle (don't remember if it was a box) heading into our yard from the direction of the river, and then heading back in the other direction a short time later.  I was trying to stay hands-off, heeding wildlife experts' advice that trying to "help" them could get them disoriented, but after a passing motorist tried to "rescue" her from the road by placing her back on OUR side, I decided it would be better to assist after all, and I took her back to the side of the road she was trying to get to.  Hopefully she recognized the terrain and navigated back to the river from there; I don't think there were any other roads in her way.  She did seem to be going the right way before I lost sight of her.

text_content: I have moved multiple box turtles off the road in the direction they were facing- or if that direction looks dangerous, into nearby woods. Recently I stopped for a 6" male that had a 1" piece of shell broken off and hanging. The wound was dirty and festering a little. He was on a super steep dangerous part of Hwy 52 so I brought him home, cleaned and treated the wound, and glued the shell shard back in place with cyanoacrylate. That held nicely, and I am monitoring him to ensure infection doesnt grow. I made him a 6' outdoor pen with food, water, shade, shelter, and plants. He sure sleeps a lot, and I have not observed him eating, so maybe he is sad, or in pain, or both. Soon as the wound heals fully I will release him (back near where I found him if I can find a safe spot). Thanks for an excellent video!

text_content: Fabulous critter! When I was a kid in Northern Virginia in the early 60s, we would see them all the time when we were exploring in the woods. I made a "farm" for them in our backyard to save as many as I could from habitat (and of course their direct) destruction by graders for new housing developments nearby. When we had to move due to my father's reassignment, we relocated our "turtles" (tortoises) to less threatened areas. Interesting factoid, at least in my experience, if you face them into a tree and go off and do your other adventures in the woods, including finding more of them, and come back they'll still be there faced into the tree! I don't think they have much of a sense of reverse! As this video creator suggested, very gentle and even affectionate souls. Tide for my favorite reptile... the other discovered in our eventual move to the Southwest being the desert horned lizard, aka "horny toad"-- same thing, character-ful, gentle souls.

text_content: I had a snapping turtle that was rescued from our parking lot on a hot summer day as a fresh hatchling, about the size of a small coin.  I raised her and handled her every day for years.  Her and I would go all over.  She got so used to people that she was like a puppy, with Petsmart being one of her favorite places.  Usually trying to climb on my shoulder and then from a shopping cart (with padding) when she got too big, trying to get a look at everything.  I had so much trust in her that even when she was big I would try to give her as much free roam time around the house as she wanted.  She usually ended up exploring some then coming back to me, try and dig into my side a bit and fall asleep.  Saying that though, I would never recommend one as a pet to anybody.  Mine was a sweety but it took me years of spending hours with her every day for her to become tame.  And all it takes is 1 bite to mess you up pretty bad for life.

text_content: Great video on explaining your feeding set-up.  I'm in Texas and have multiple Ornate's and agree 100% that they should be kept outside.  Hibernation and all.  I was interested in your enclosure walls - they are not electrified and seem to have to "deterrent" for predators.  Where I am, my enclosure is under constant watch by skunks and armadillo's.  They don't want to eat the adults, but dig up my nests.  For the longest time I thought it was a racoon, but after installing night vision video camera's, low and behold, it was skunks and even armadillo's.  I could not figure out how they were getting in and after moving the camera's around and painstakingly watching them (8 hours of nightime video watching is boring....), I finally figured it out and stopped it - but the damage was done.  I used to keep the babies in the main enclosure but exactly like you said, they were getting picked off by hawks.  Again, the vidoe camera revealed all.  So, back my question - no deterants?  Also, do you now attract vermin like rats etc bu leaving ANY food out at night?  I used to and after watching rats and opposums eat the food, I stopped doing that.

text_content: We found a tiny turtle 4 years ago on my air force base's flightline.  We  bought a 10 gal tank and kept him enslaved.  He got a bit bigger so we upgraded to a 20 gal tank.  I haven't been able to 100% identify what kind of turtle he is but I'm fairly certain he's a Florida Mud Turtle as we're in Florida.  Somehow he's been living in the shop for over 4 years despite none of us knowing how to properly care for him.  Anyway,  what is the preferred diet for Mud Turtles?  We've tried a great many things but he doesn't seem to eat most of it.  Things he does like are a certain brand of Pleco Wafers for sucker fish, sometimes he eats full sized frozen shrimp. he'll eat Mole-Crickets when we catch them and toss em in the tank.  His absolute favorite was pond snails one of the guys brought in, he ate 45 of them within 2 hours.  Snail-mageddon!  I've bought many different "turtle foods" from amazon but most of them he wont eat.

text_content: While planting an avocado tree in my back yard, I accidentally dug up and damaged a turtle egg. I could see the fully formed guy inside as part of the eggshell had come off. Then I found another egg but this one was undamaged. I brought them inside and kept them moist. The remaining damaged eggshell fell away after a couple of days and there he was, a Florida three striped mud turtle. It's been nearly a week but I can't get him to eat anything. Hopefully soon. I'll have to see if I can find some duckweed. His yoke sack fell off after two days so at least he got some nutrition from that. In the meantime the other egg remains unhatched. This isn't my first rodeo with turtle hatchlings. About three years ago I had accidentally dug up a cooter nest just as they were hatching while I was planting a hibiscus bush. I kept them until they were almost fully grown and then released the three of them into the fresh water canal out behind my house.

text_content: Feeding goldfish and Rosie's will eventually kill your turtles. They have a chemical called thiaminase which will build up in the turtle's system and when it gets bad it will display as neurological issues with the turtles. It blocks vitamin B12, and a heavy dose of B12 can fix it if noticed soon enough. My advice is to Google fish that don't contain thiaminase and use those, you can even buy frozen fish, thaw them out and chop them up and the turtles won't see any difference. Also I love how you did an elaborate setup for very common turtles, you're putting people to shame! Many species need different types of cages or temperatures so please keep that in mind. My side job is reptiles in general, monitor lizards and snakes (dozens of species) if you ever need more specific info hit me up. Check out Fox Reptiles if you are ever in Ohio and need a cool video with an insanely rare pretty ass monitor lizard or something. --Steve

text_content:
This was an amazing introduction to the different types of tortoises and turtles out there, but also because you talk about responsibility in the first few minutes! I own a Russian (Morty the Tortie) and a Red-footed (Yurtle the Turtle) and respect them both so much as amazing reptiles! Russians don't need just a clear enclosure to not understand boundaries. Our Morty is in a dark enclosure and when he wants to get out for a walk, will repeatedly bang, scratch, and try to climb out. One thing I think you could have hit on with the Russian is that they need lots of exercise and/or a nice big enclosure, because in the wild they impressively walk 4 MILES A DAY (pretty impressive when you take their size into account!) Their also speed demons! My guy can run off in a flash, so you have to be careful to not lose sight of them. 

A big pro is their amazing personalities! Morty the Tortie reminds me of a grumpy old man who is actually lovable under their hard shell! He's very social and so smart! They're amazingly great pets, but just like you, I wish pet stores would not sell wild caught and teach buyers that they need a big enclosurer of about 6 square feet. 

Thanks so much for teaching responsibility in reptile ownership, something rare for channels because they are also breeding them. Also, I LOVE that you spoke about rescuing sulcatas. Amazing video! I subscribed instantly!

video_title: How to Setup a Turtle Tank
text_content:
If I ever get a pet turtle I can tell you for a fact it’s not going to be a red eared slider. I refuse to keep an invasive species that is going to end up destroying the populations of native turtles. text_content: Wow I love how much you love to teach and share....subscribed! I rescued another turtle today during my run and this time there was a fence there and I was trying to find somewhere I could pull the fence out to get her in and right when I found it a guy yelled and said he had a place inside his area where he could slide it under. Fortunately, since I knew water comes out of the shell when I lifted it over my head to hand it to him I did not get any water on me and I was wondering how dirty it was and whether it could also be pee...Then I found you and although it didn't address my original reason for searching I am glad I found you so I can learn more. When in the Marines (long ago) I first encountered a snapping turtle and fortunately an officer rushed to me to tell me to be careful. Being from Chicago how would I know a turtle could snap and BAM it went for the stick the officer held out and I have never forgotten. text_content: I appreciate that you pointed out that there are plenty of these turtles already in need of homes. One thing I wish you had added was the reason why these turtles are actually illegal in some places. Here in the Pacific Northwest, red eared-sliders are invasive, competing against our native painted and western pond turtles. People will either intentionally let their pets loose, or they will put their pets in a pond or stream in their yard, and it's not too hard for them to find their way to other bodies of water. Like you said, they are really hardy reptiles, which unfortunately makes them really good at establishing invasive populations, so it is important for people to be aware of just how hardy (and potentially harmful) they can be.

text_content: There’s a lot of misinformation here. Especially in the photos and videos shown. There’s a couple turtles with severe metabolic bone disease (shells curling up). Pictures of turtles being offered food out of water (they have to eat in water or they will choke). There is a photo of a turtle with a severely overgrown beak. Many of the photos/videos you show are not even red eared sliders. You responded to someone’s comment about a serious size difference in their 2 red eared sliders. It’s not a good idea to house more than 1 together because they will compete for resources and one could be winning (the dominant one). That’s not everything wrong with the video. Please don’t put out animal care videos if you don’t do the proper research.

text_content: Story time for anybody interested:I saved a turtle's life, so 45 minutes ago, my sister was riding her scooter around the street and she found a very small turtle, on the side of the road, and for context, the nearest pond is a 45 minute walk there, it was roughly the size of the palm of my hand, we had a bunch of cats so I didn't want to risk the cats messing with the poor lil guy, so me and my sister went to the pond near the park and as I held it, I think it started to get used to me as it was moving slowly, when I set it down near the water, it took it a few moments before it went into the water, burying under some seagrass, still trying to process that incredible moment, I'm not sure if it came from the pond of if it was someones pet they either lost, hopefully not abandoned, but I will forever cherish that moment.

text_content: We are caring for a southwest desert tortoise for a couple more weeks. He has gotten very restless and testing all barriers. He eats grass and tortoise food but seems less interested in eating. We’ve had rain so I thought maybe he senses the monsoon and is seeking high ground? We give him a big water dish to go in and have a container we soak him in in the afternoons when it’s been 115. When he first came he was happy to eat for hours on grass, but he has gotten so restless he isn’t eating much. I’d like to give him other food but not sure what he is ok to eat other than the occasional strawberry. He lives outdoors at the desert botanical garden in his own little yard and we have him in our backyard. But it has been HOT! He has a burrow we made him that is shaded as well and frequently overnights outside of his burrow. He will go home to the garden after they are finished with the remodel of the house attached to his yard. But I just want to make sure he is as happy as possible.

video_description: tortoise #gardenstatetortoise #babytortoise The red foot tortoise is historically one of the most beloved tortoise species in the world ...
text_content: A few years ago, a red footed tortoise just showed up in our south Jersey backyard, we found him eating cat food we leave out for a feral.  I thought he was some huge mutant box turtle - who would think an exotic species would just wander into your yard?  Either someone dumped him, or he escaped from an outside enclosure.  He ate all the cat food, and we put out some more, which he also ate, then he spent the night in our flower bed.  The next day I showed a picture of him to my brother, who identified him as a red foot, and I rushed home, hoping he was still snoozing in the garden, and thank goodness he was.  I called a few turtle people in the area, but got the impression from some that if they took him, he might then get sold. I contacted Garden State Tortoise and Chris agreed to give him a home.  My niece and I took the 40 minute drive to drop him off and asked Chris if we could see a little of the place when we got there.  I don’t think it is open to the public, but Chris was so nice and so gracious and showed us around.  It was such a relief to know the tortoise would be living in what is pretty much tortoise heaven, and that he would have other tortoises of his species around, and someone knowledgeable, and responsible, to look out for him.  I hope he is doing well.  Thanks so much, Chris, for all you do!

text_content: I've had a tortoise rob in my family for 50 years now. My grandpa found him as a baby with a yolk Sac near the mountains when he was 15. He roams in my grandpa's backyard and gets to live in nature. Remember if you keep him enclosed the tortoises life span will naturally decrease. In nature they can live for way longer. He enjoys the sunlight , drinks water from ponds and after a long day in the burrow he might sit with my grandpa at the patio. He eats once a day at best because they somehow seem to conserve their energy resources very efficiently. He's really quiet and has a great memory. We have a huge lettuce farm behind the yard so that's what he has been eating with occasional watermelon treats

text_content: I have a mata mata del Rio orinoco taken at a turtle fair in 2019.Now for two years she has been living in a preformed pond that I installed internally at home. I alternate frozen fish with live fish. I also tried other types of food that were never considered by her. Acidify water from time to time by adding catappa leaf. The temperature of the winter water. It is around the 24' in the hottest spot. Out of the water I only have two neon LEDs for the plants. Being that the water evaporates, the right moist condensation is created for when it comes out to breathe and I refill hot water weekly. So far I haven't had any health problems and it has reached a fair size. Now that she is grown I feed her twice a week, but sometimes I leave fish in the pond that last several times/days and that she loves to 😁😁 hunt. As a child, however, she ate almost every day. But even as a child there was no way to make them eat worms, insects or other food, she preferred to starve rather even though on some groups they said she could eat anything other than fish. At the moment I have never included heated outdoor lamps even though the room is not particularly warm in winter. I use a pond filter pump with a uvc lamp and the inserted plants also help to keep the water clean.

I'm Brazilian and if this turtle genuinely came from the Amazon forest and was not raised in captivity it's possible that it was illegally trafficked. In Brazil, the sale and possession of Mata-Mata turtles are illegal according to the Environmental Crimes Law (Law No. 9,605/1998), and the Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources (IBAMA) also regulates the possession and trade of native species. I highly recommend you guys to research where this fish store sourced this turtle!!!

The sale and trade of Mata-Mata turtles for abroad can be legal in some cases, as long as all applicable laws and regulations are followed, both in the country of origin and the destination country. To legally export a Mata-Mata turtle as a pet, the exporter must obtain a CITES export permit from the country of origin and ensure that all local laws related to the possession and trade of wildlife are respected. In addition, the importer must ensure that the trade and possession of the turtle are legal in the destination country and, if necessary, obtain a CITES import permit or other relevant authorizations. You should ask the pet store for documentation regarding the origin of the Mata-Mata turtle and any required permits, such as CITES import permits or any other relevant authorizations.

video_title: BURMESE PYTHON !! Keeping one as a PET !
video_description: Thanks for stopping by, Subscribe and like Patreon : https://www.patreon.com/Ben_cincysnakes Instagram : Ben CincySnakes ...
text_content:
Beautiful burm!  I love Burmese pythons. Its a shame that what people see on the news about Florida is all that most people will ever know about them. 

Theyve been a favorite of mine since i was a kid. Long story short, I wanted an African rock python for my 10th birthday (i had been keeping ball pythons and other snakes for years) but got a Burmese python instead. 

Now im in my mid 30s and keep Retics and Afrocks too. They are all so different but i love them all. 

Back in the day Burms were easily the most popular of the 3, but holy cow have Retics taken over lately! Hardly anyone keeps Burms and even fewer keep Afrocks these days.

 IMO Burms are the most practical for most people. Although they are more prone to respiratory issues. 
Afrocks are hardier for sure, but require a better understanding of snake behavior when it comes to handling them. They are NOT mean, but they are less forgiving than burms or retics on average. If you put the time in though, they are the most interesting to work with by far. Even smarter than retics believe it or not! more powerful and athletic too! At least lb for lb.

text_content: I wish more people would give Carpet Pythons the time of day.  Irian Jaya Carpet pythons are basically as hardy as a corn snake and top out around 6ft. That was my wifes 1st snake 9 years ago and now she keeps retics and all sorts of stuff. Anyways, IJ carpet pythons have surprisingly low humidity requirements compared to other members of Morelia. They also do not need it particularly hot. Low to Mid 80s is plenty for the warm side. Unlike some carpet python localities they only really need to be misted if they are shedding. My male hasnt skipped a meal in 9 years. One of the easiest snakes i own. Even my ball python technically takes more work because i have to mist her and she likes to skip meals. It is probably true that it is harder to get bitten by a ball python than a carpet python, but my Carpet python hasnt ever bitten anyone either. He has a fantastic feeding response, but as long as you use your head its easy to manage. He is tap trained and all that too. I mostly keep Burms, retics, and an Afrock. So hes kind of like the training snake since he acts very similarly to the retics.

video_title: What to know before owning a Savannah Monitor! 🦎
video_description: Meet this incredible #savannahmonitorlizard that visited our hospital for a wellness exam! Our #exoticpet doctor, Dr. Patt explains ...
text_content:
hi, doctor patt! i keep savannah monitors. they do not do well in an arid environment…in fact the reason they burrow is not only to escape predators but because the LOVE the moisture beneath the soil. they will develop severe shedding issues if kept in a dry habitat…i have seen this firsthand and am working on rehabilitating a monitor kept like that right now. savannah monitors also have a laterally (vertically) shaped tail which heavily implies swimming abilities…they love moisture, humidity, and water! my boy goes swimming by his own choice very often. moisture also helps their burrows keep their shape. it is very enriching for them, they are not like a bearded dragon. “Immad” made this same point. everything else in this video is accurate. other damaging misconceptions about them: they are insectivores, it is a common belief that they can be fed rats. this causes fatty liver disease, their number one killer.
people underestimate the temperatures that savannah monitors need…while they are usually kept at around 90-100 for their basking spots, they fare much better at 130 degrees and higher. 
people also still believe in infrared lights, which is damaging to their eyes and causes burns. i have seen this firsthand also. it is not effective and they do way better on full-spectrum lighting. 
most people also think that savannah monitors are easy, low-maintenance pets. this line of thinking kills savannah monitors! they are very expensive despite their initial price tag of $20-$40.

video_title: Feeding a Savannah Monitor! 🦎 Why These Lizards Go Wild for Food😅
video_description: Feeding time with my Savannah monitor is always an adventure In this video, you'll see just how wild these lizards can get ...
text_content:
This is exactly why most people do not understand the correct way to keep Savannah monitors ! It’s because of Videos like this one giving the wrong information ! Just for the record…I’ve been keeping Savannahs for 5-6yrs now…& that’s 5-6yrs of doing research and taking care of them the correct way ! There is absolutely no reason why any monitors especially Savannahs that are strictly insectivores… Are being fed a grocery store diet… to include ground turkey, chicken gizzards or chicken legs which is what this poor Savannah was being fed in this video…. Which I’m sure is the main reason why it’s overweight  ! Whole pray items are the best to feed monitors & it’s actually cheaper to do so ! Not to mention, way more healthier for the animal ! 
Savannahs are strictly insectivore & in the wild they are mainly eating the large African land snails, millipedes & whatever other insects they can find. If fed the incorrect diet… like grocery store items..Savannahs & other monitors Will develop fatty liver disease pretty quick & we live a very short life 😢😣
If anyone is wanting a Savannah… please do your research first & get the correct care information. Don’t take notes from videos like this ! 
Your Savannah will thank you !

video_title: Buying Golden Mantella Females for my Breeding Project (Mantella Aurantiaca)
video_description: Join my trip to the local pet shop where they have managed to source some female Mantella for me after a long search.
text_content:
That would be good if you got them to be able to breed under your care. Apparently they're under tremendous pressures from over collection and rainforest destruction in Madagascar. I am not suggesting that your frogs were victims of an animal crime ring or anything, like importation of endangered animals illegally... no judgement. It doesn't matter. What matters is they went somewhere good - you! ✌🏽

I just happened to see a documentary; children from villages go "herping", albeit not as a westerner would, lol. They catch the frogs (of various species) for money, along with chameleons & geckos. And, if they can do it without the mother noticing, baby lemurs. The reptiles & amphibians are to be thrown haphazardly into the child's bag, and this is after being (fairly violently) pursued and captured.  Chameleons aren't carefully coerced out of treetops, onto a pole to grab, like they do in Florida; no, the kids just knock 'em to the ground. Hm. You'd think theyd be in trouble for this from the ringleader right? I'd think they would be more careful knowing that their meager income is involved. Lol. And the kids sometimes "casually" swing the bags around as they skip home. Nice. But before home, a quick stop - go to the local animal dealer (aka importer) and he pays them one cent for each animal. I've never heard of more than one "shilling" or dollar being paid to these kids. Then the kids are paid and sent off; the reptiles are separated, and the lot of them are packed according to species, one on top of the other, and shipped internationally. It's really the worst for the chameleons, as they can't stand being social at all really. Let alone looked at haha. And they're just sensitive period. None of this was relating to your frogs by the way I was just appalled by it. Haha.

text_content: I genuinely appreciate the frog exposure, we need more of it. But it’s not accurate information. The frog you’re showing in the pictures is Oophaga histrionica, the “red head” locality. Also known as the harlequin poison frog, AKA a frog, not a toad. Not to be confused with harlequin toads, which are true toads and are from the “Atelopus” genus (which I personally work very closely with, specifically Atelopus barbotini). The animal you’re referring to is neither. You’re likely referring to Oophaga anchicayensis, also known as the Anchicaya poison frog. Which is not a toad, nor is it the animal shown of the pictures. Though being from the same genus (that being Oophaga), it’s very closely related to the frog shown on the pictures. Your information on the pet trade is also not completely accurate. Though unfortunately a lot of frogs are still illegally imported, there’s a lot of people working in the herpetoculture and vivarium hobby to minimise and prevent that where possible. Facilities like Tesoros de Colombia are working legally and responsibly with many poison dart frog and harlequin toad species, to both provide animals for the hobby and help populations in the wild, through release and conservation. Shipments are done properly. And the people in the hobby working with these animals know very well how to take care of them, unlike what you said in the video. Which can be proven, since hobbyists managed to breed many of these species way before zoos and conservationists even got close. Two notable examples being Dendrobates tinctorius and Atelopus barbotini (toads from the Atelopus genus specifically being incredibly hard to breed and everything needing to be exactly perfect for them to do so), the latter having not a single stable breeding population outside of the hobby, since many conservation facilities don’t work with them. Thus making the responsible and legal herpetoculture hobby extremely important for the future of these amazing animals. It’s unfortunate our hobby and conservation work gets put in such a bad light by animal rights extremists, would be nice to see some people actually doing the research themselves and spreading the amazing work and effort people in our hobby are putting into breeding and keeping these very delicate little animals, which great success (again, before zoos and conservationists ever succeeded or even tried). Hope you learned something. Keep up the passion for animals and conservation!! :)

text_content:
Next time when feeding, you need to coat the bugs with calcium D3 powder to prevent Metabolic Bone Disease which is caused from calcium deficiency, or a lack of calcium. The calcium is important for the well being of the bones, nerve and muscle functions, and without it, can cause deformities, and even death.

Please do not mix dart frog species or morphs. All frogs must be the same morph and species to prevent territorialism and aggression which can lead to both frogs attacking, and potentially harming each other. Different morphs or species being housed together can also cause them hybridize which may mess up genes.

For the substrate, use natural substrate like coco fiber or reptisoil. The substrate in this video is just pebbles which can be used for a drainage layer at the bottom for water, but not good for a top layer.

While you did add in leaf litter, a bit of moss, such as spagma moss on the ground to maintain humidity in the vivarium would be a good idea.

Try not to add too much ventilation. Yes, a little bit of ventilation is good, but giving too much can cause both the enclosure, and the frogs to dry out. You can try covering up the top screen a bit, and leave a small amount of ventilation.

Please do not handle your frogs. Me saying this is not me caring too much about these animals, but me giving advice. Holding your dart frogs wont cause a bond, but will cause stress to the frog. These animals are skittish, and will flee when feeling threatened. Attempting to chase and grab it will only cause stress. All amphibians absorb liquids like water through their skin, and handling your frog can transfer oils, salt, and substances on your hand to the frog which can be harmful, so please try to refrain frog holding your dart frog from now on.

text_content:
Try to simulate their natural environment as far as you can. Including morning mist and rain. Africa is hot, but it also has the day-night cycle of moisture and heat coolness, which are important for the frogs.
These frogs also lay their eggs inside the soil and guard them, so avoid digging them up and touching them at all costs. Before breeding, try to create a bioactive container, so you can rest easy with the soils not going bad and enduring the moisture levels and the frogs (and their waste) themself better. Their skin is delicate, so the hard and sharp gravel in the tank is not the best possible choice. The noises the frogs make might be because of breeding. Frogs usually make noise to attract a mate.  Please keep on reporting how things are going!

text_content:
I used to work at a chytrid research lab.  (Almost caused a leak once 😅)

You’d be surprised how much the disease has spread.  Where I live a lot of the native leopard and red legged frogs are getting pushed out of their natural habitat by bullfrogs, which spread the disease but are more resistant to it.  

Whenever you get salamanders or frogs keep them isolated until test results come back and do an inspection for light brown mottling on their sides, mottling or redness on the stomach, and a damage around the mouth. Pet distributors are one of the biggest ways Chytrid (Ki-trid) can spread.

text_content: Having specialists keep animals for pets actually can help figure out why certain populations might be struggling and how to help them (emphasis on specialists). The figuring out how to simulate when they know to feed is genius and things like someone down further suggesting breeding maybe has been unsuccessful because they lay eggs deeper than normal burrows can be clues as well for where to look if something is going on (for example if a population doesn't seem to be laying eggs). It's a lot harder than people think to just observe them in the wild... there's so much we don't even know about animals that are everywhere (like crows and all their practices as an example) and if you can simulate their environment you understand their environment. I definitely don't think your average person should keep these as pets but this is a fascinating attempt to figure these little guys out. There's also the fact that if you can breed it you can save it.

text_content:
Hello, I'm commenting as a Japanese person. 
Japanese macaques are known as apes that live in the northernmost cold regions of the world, mainly in scrub forests and rocky mountains. They live in groups, constantly moving through the forests, eating mulberry, oak, chestnut and other fruits. They are very active, and when there is no food in the forest, they all come down to the villages and attack crops in the fields and people, destroying persimmons and other fruits. There are many comments from viewers saying they want to adopt Punchi as a pet, but they are always happiest living in a group, and are by no means animals that are suited to being kept as pets. Also, during the breeding season, their entire faces and rears turn bright red, and they do not look very clean, so they are not creatures that can be easily controlled by humans, and I think they are best suited to living in nature or in an enclosure ♪ 
Punch-kun will have experienced many challenges there and will surely one day grow into a strong leader of that group. 😉

text_content: I was sitting in a deer stand one evening when a family of raccoons showed up.  There was a feeder in a nearby tree hung from a limb by a rope.  This feeder was a big can with a motorized device set to disperse corn at only certain times of the day.  The mother raccoon climbed the tree, walked down the limb and slid down the rope to the top of the feeder.  She then proceeded to swing the feeder Tarzan style until it banged against the trunk of the tree multiple times, spilling corn from the feeder each time.  After she'd gotten enough to feed her family, she reversed the process and they all dined on a huge pile of corn.

text_content:
Mule deer have also adapted to life in cities in Canada. The does spend almost all of their time in rural towns since there’s a lot of food and bears, cougars, and coyotes tend to stay away or get dealt with. This has lead to generations of mule deer growing up in and living around humans, to the point where we know when to be on our toes when walking our dogs. In Spring, does become very protective of their fawns when non-deer quadrupeds are involved and will step up if you get too close. October-November is when the bucks roll in when they’re in rut, so you either steer clear or carry a stick (or have a mean right hook) to save yourself or your dog an expensive lesson.

I swear, I’ve seen these deer waiting for a red light before crossing the street, they’re that habituated.
